primary_decisions_supported: Headcount planning for Customer Success and Support
Resource allocation to segments for retention
executive_questions_answered: Given our customer count and usage, how many CSMs/Support agents do we need to maintain service levels?
Are our current CSMs managing too many accounts (risking churn) or do we have capacity to handle more growth?
How will expansions in customer base or usage translate to required increases in CS/support staffing?

metric_definitions.definition: The average number of customer accounts managed per Customer Success Manager. Helps gauge workload and ability to provide adequate attention (e.g., high-touch vs tech-touch).
metric_definitions.formula: Accounts per CSM = Total number of active customer accounts / Total number of CSMs
required_dimensions: customer segment (if varying ratios by segment)

known_variants.definition_delta: Total ARR managed per CSM, which can normalize differing account sizes (one CSM might handle $5M ARR vs another $2M)
known_variants.when_to_use: When account sizes vary widely and workload correlates more with revenue than count

pitfalls: Doesn't reflect complexity differences (10 small customers might be easier than 2 huge ones even if count suggests otherwise)
If CSMs have support from other roles (technical account managers, etc.), pure count might mislead – consider whole team capacity
Ignoring growth in existing accounts: same account might require more effort as it grows or if it's in onboarding vs steady state

metric_definitions.definition: The volume of support tickets (or interactions) handled per support agent in a given period. Used to gauge support workload and whether staffing is sufficient to meet SLA/response times.
metric_definitions.formula: Tickets per Agent = Total support tickets in period / Number of support agents
required_dimensions: ticket severity or channel if relevant

known_variants.definition_delta: Not a capacity metric directly, but an outcome metric that ties to capacity – average time to first response. Indirectly indicates if capacity is sufficient.
known_variants.when_to_use: Tracking service level health in conjunction with capacity metrics

pitfalls: High tickets per agent could indicate overload, but also consider resolution complexity (not all tickets equal effort)
Seasonality in support volume could temporarily skew ratio – use proper period averages for planning
Automation and self-service can change this ratio without headcount changes (e.g., introducing a chatbot might lower tickets/agent by deflecting some issues)

joins_and_keys.notes: Assign each account to a CSM to count accounts per CSM
Aggregate tickets by account or directly by support team load

transformation_rules: Filter to active paying customers for account counts (exclude churned or prospects)
If one account has multiple CSMs (unusual, but enterprise might), decide whether to split count or assign primary CSM only
Consider different weighting: e.g., count a large enterprise account as equivalent of 5 SMB accounts when calculating capacity

data_quality_checks.logic: Every active account should have an assigned CSM in the data
Check ticket counts over time for anomalies (if using for forecasting, sudden dips/spikes explained by data issues or real events)

aggregation_rules: Typically aggregated at team or segment level: e.g., Enterprise team accounts/CSM vs SMB team accounts/CSM
Use averages or medians as needed (if distribution is skewed, median accounts per CSM might be informative)
calculations_and_models.cohort_logic: Could cohort customers by onboarding date to anticipate support load (new customers create more tickets initially)
calculations_and_models.forecast_logic: Given projected customer growth (or usage), forecast needed CSMs or agents to maintain target ratio. E.g., if adding 100 accounts and target 20 accounts/CSM, need +5 CSMs.

layout.purpose: Show current workload per CSM in different customer segments vs targets
Track how many tickets each support agent handles and if that's rising or falling
annotations_or_thresholds: SLA thresholds (if any, e.g., 5 tickets/day per agent for complex issues)

narrative_insights_to_generate: Identify if any team is over capacity (e.g., Enterprise CSMs each handle 30 accounts vs target 15) and potential impact on retention
Highlight whether support ticket load per agent is trending up, indicating need for more hires or process improvements
diagnostic_questions: Are high account-to-CSM ratios correlating with lower NRR or CSAT in those segments?
Is the support team maintaining response times given current tickets per agent, or are SLAs slipping?

recommended_actions.trigger_condition: Accounts per CSM exceeds target (e.g., >X accounts) in high-touch segments, risking quality of engagement
Tickets per Agent climbing steadily, approaching a point where response SLAs are at risk or agent burnout likely

implementation_notes.minimum_viable_version: Annual capacity review: static calculation of current accounts per CSM and tickets per agent, compared to last year
implementation_notes.advanced_version: Real-time capacity dashboard with predictive alerts (e.g., if sales closes Y new accounts, how CSM capacity will look in 3 months; if support volume trend continues, when agents will exceed threshold)
common_client_customizations: Different targets for different tiers (e.g., 1 CSM per 2 enterprise accounts vs 1 per 50 SMB accounts) visualized separately
Include quality metrics like CSAT or renewal rates alongside to correlate capacity and outcomes
privacy_and_access_notes: Aggregate metrics; individual CSM or agent performance might be sensitive – keep focus on averages unless needed for managerial review
This data is internal. If shared externally (unlikely), ensure no sensitive internal thresholds are exposed
success_criteria: No surprise churn spikes or support escalations due to overworked teams – capacity model accurately predicted needs
Service levels (response times, CSAT) maintained as customer base grows
